era_context: The Roman Empire under Hadrian (r. 117–138 CE) is at its territorial zenith and in the midst of cultural and architectural patronage. The Bar Kokhba Revolt in Judaea is ongoing. The Eastern Han dynasty in China is experiencing increasing court instability and eunuch influence. The Kushan Empire dominates Central Asia and the Silk Road.

events.title: Bar Kokhba Revolt: continuation and Roman military response
events.description: The Bar Kokhba Revolt, which began in 132 CE, continues into 133 CE with Jewish rebels controlling significant territory in Judaea. Roman forces under Sextus Julius Severus conduct a systematic campaign to suppress the rebellion, employing siege warfare and scorched-earth tactics. The revolt is characterized by intense fighting, with both sides suffering heavy casualties. By 133 CE, the rebellion is in its second year, and Roman military pressure is intensifying, though the final suppression will not occur until 134–135 CE.
events.certainty_note: The Bar Kokhba Revolt's continuation into 133 CE is well-attested in multiple sources. The dating of the revolt's phases is secure.
cross_references: Part of the Bar Kokhba Revolt (132–135 CE)

events.title: Hadrian's travels: visit to Egypt and the Nile
events.description: Hadrian, known for his extensive travels throughout the empire, visits Egypt in 133 CE (or possibly 130–131 CE; sources vary). During his visit, he travels along the Nile, visits ancient temples, and participates in religious ceremonies. The death of his beloved companion Antinous occurs during this journey (traditionally dated to 130 CE, though some sources place it in 133 CE). Antinous is deified by Hadrian, and numerous cities and temples are founded in his honor. This event reflects Hadrian's cultural interests and his role as a patron of Hellenistic culture.
key_figures: Antinous (Hadrian's companion, deified after his death)

sources.name: Scriptores Historiae Augustae (Historia Augusta), Life of Hadrian
Archaeological evidence from Egypt and temples dedicated to Antinous

events.certainty_note: Hadrian's Egyptian journey is well-attested, but the exact dating is debated. Most sources place it c. 130–131 CE, though some place it in 133 CE. The death of Antinous is traditionally dated to 130 CE, but the exact year is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of Hadrian's broader travels throughout the empire
Reflects the cultural and religious dimensions of Roman imperial power

events.title: Eastern Han court: continued factional struggles and eunuch influence
events.description: The Eastern Han court continues to be dominated by factional struggles between eunuchs, imperial consorts' families, and scholar-officials. By 133 CE, the pattern of child emperors and regents is well-established, with eunuchs wielding increasing power. The imperial succession remains unstable, and court intrigues are frequent. This period marks the continuation of the institutional decline that will eventually lead to the dynasty's collapse in 220 CE.

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled 5th century CE

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of court instability and eunuch ascendancy in the 2nd century CE is well-established. The specific year 133 CE is assigned by convention; the exact chronology of individual court events is less certain.
cross_references: Part of the decline of the Eastern Han dynasty
Contemporary with Roman political stability under Hadrian (contrast)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Hadrian's Egyptian journey and the death of Antinous is disputed. Most modern scholars place these events in 130 CE, not 133 CE. The traditional dating of Antinous's death to 130 CE is based on numismatic and epigraphic evidence, though some sources suggest alternative dates. The significance of Antinous's deification is sometimes overstated in popular accounts; while it was unusual, it was not unprecedented in the Roman world.
historiographic_note: The year 133 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire, with multiple sources available for major events. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. For China, the historical record is rich but filtered through later compilations; contemporary records from 133 CE itself are sparse. The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no synchronized written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the Bar Kokhba Revolt
The ongoing revolt in 133 CE leads to its final suppression in 135 CE
Hadrian's Egyptian journey may be contemporary with or follow the death of Antinous
